risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A stratified-block randomization method was used to assign all eligible persons to one of the intervention arms, stratified by BMI and gender." Comment: This explicitly describes a stratified block randomization procedure, which is a proper random allocation approach and is sufficient to rate sequence generation as low risk.

Quote: "A stratified-block randomization method was used to assign all eligible persons to one of the intervention arms, stratified by BMI and gender." Comment: The report describes the randomization sequence and stratification, but gives no explicit information about how allocation was concealed from the person enrolling participants (e.g., central allocation or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes). Therefore foreknowledge of the next assignment cannot be ruled out.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "IDEA was a single-blind, single-center, 18-month, randomized controlled trial" Comment: The study was only single-blind, and the intervention arms (diet, diet + exercise, exercise) were delivered in person with obvious behavioral differences, so participants and personnel delivering the interventions were very unlikely to be blinded. Because the main outcomes include pain and physical function, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence these outcomes, making performance bias high overall.

Quote: "The trial was ... single-blind" and "The following MRI features were scored ... MRIs were read paired and unblinded to timepoint by a musculoskeletal radiologist". Comment: Outcome assessment for the key structural MRI outcomes was performed by a radiologist who was explicitly unblinded to timepoint, and there is no indication that allocation was blinded for the outcome assessor. The assessed outcomes include semi-quantitative MRI scores that involve judgment and could be influenced by knowledge of treatment/timepoint, so lack of blinding could bias measurement.

Quote: "A total of 454 persons..." and "All radiographs with paired acquisitions (baseline and FU18) are included in this analysis (n = 325)." and Table I footnote: "105 persons had baseline and 18 months MRIs of whom 98 had adequate quality images for the purposes of analysis." Comment: The paper clearly indicates substantial but not excessive missingness for the imaging subsamples, with complete paired X-rays for the X-ray analysis and only a small number of MRI scans excluded for inadequate quality (98 of 105). There is no signal that missing data were imbalanced by group or related to outcomes, and the analyses presented use the available paired follow-up data. Overall, attrition/missing outcome data are unlikely to have biased the results.
